title: Your Brain Is Not Capable of Randomization

lines.text: Chapter one, section ten. Your Brain Is Not Capable of Randomization.

We've established that mixed strategies — randomizing your choices with specific probabilities — are sometimes essential for reaching an equilibrium, particularly in zero-sum situations like most poker scenarios.
But there's a serious practical challenge: human brains are genuinely terrible at generating true randomness.
This section of the book addresses that challenge directly, and the advice it gives is more important than it might initially seem.
Let's say you've determined that when facing a bet on the river with a certain bluff-catcher, you should call fifty percent of the time.

Maybe the math tells you that your opponent's bluffing frequency makes calling and folding exactly equal in EV — you're indifferent — and so you've decided to call half the time and fold the other half.
How do you actually implement that?
One approach that seems intuitive but is completely wrong: you tell yourself that you folded the last time you were in this spot, so you'll call this time.
That way, over two situations, you've called once and folded once, achieving a fifty-fifty split.
The book explains why this is flawed in multiple ways.
First, the previous situation was almost certainly not the same situation.
The board was different, the action leading up to it was different, the opponent might have been different, your hand was different.
Even if it looked similar on the surface, the underlying variables were different, which means the correct strategy might have been different too.
You can't treat two distinct hands as instances of the same situation just because they superficially resembled each other.
Second, and more fundamentally, this approach makes your behavior predictable in exactly the way you're trying to avoid.
Your opponent knows that you folded the last time they made this kind of bet.
If you have a policy of alternating — fold one time, call the next — then your opponent can predict that you'll call this time.
And if they can predict your action, they can exploit it.
A person who knew you alternated could time their bluffs to coincide with your 'fold' turns and their value bets to coincide with your 'call' turns.
You would have completely undermined the point of randomizing.

Third, the book points out that even in the hypothetical where you found yourself in precisely identical situations back to back — same board, same action, same opponent, same hand — the correct approach is still to treat each instance as fully independent.
Each time you're in the situation, there must be a fifty percent chance you call, regardless of what happened last time.
The past is not relevant.
If what you did last time influences what you do this time, that's a pattern, and patterns are predictable.
The book uses a beautiful analogy to make this point: think about flipping a fair coin.
The probability of heads on any given flip is exactly fifty percent.
That never changes based on previous flips.
If you flipped heads three times in a row, the probability of heads on the fourth flip is still exactly fifty percent.
Each flip is an independent event.
You cannot and should not try to predict the next flip based on the history of previous flips.
Your poker decisions need to work the same way.
When you're in a spot where you should call fifty percent of the time, each occurrence of that spot needs its own independent randomization, completely unaffected by what you did last time.
So if alternating and remembering previous hands are wrong approaches, what's the right one?
Use an external random number generator.
The book offers several practical options.
If you're playing live and have a digital watch or clock with a seconds display, you can glance at the last digit before acting.
If it's zero, one, two, three, or four — that is, less than five — you call.
If it's five, six, seven, eight, or nine — five or above — you fold.
That gives you exactly a fifty percent chance of calling, determined by a number that neither you nor your opponent can predict or control.
If you're playing online, a coin flip is perfectly workable.
For different probability splits — say you need to call thirty percent of the time — you could roll a ten-sided die and call on a result of one, two, or three.
There are also websites and apps that generate true random numbers on demand, like Random.org.
The key principle is that the randomization device needs to be genuinely unpredictable and outside your control.
Your own thought process does not qualify.

Human intuitions about randomness are notoriously unreliable — we systematically overestimate how often patterns should 'break,' we're influenced by recency, and we have unconscious biases toward certain numbers or choices.
Multiple experiments in psychology have demonstrated that when people try to generate random sequences, the sequences they produce have statistical patterns that a simple algorithm could detect.
We're not wired for true randomness.

The implication for poker is direct and practical: if your game-theoretically correct strategy requires you to call with a hand some fraction of the time and fold the rest of the time, find a mechanical randomization method before you sit down.
Decide in advance what your randomization device will be and how you'll interpret its output.
Do not rely on your own sense of what 'feels random' in the moment, and do not rely on what you did in previous hands.
The goal of randomization is to become unpredictable to your opponent.
Anything that introduces a pattern — however subtle — defeats that goal.
